title: Transurethral anatomical enucleation of the prostate using a 1470-nm diode laser (DiLEP) versus transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) for large-volume benign prostatic hyperplasia: a prospective randomized controlled trial.
abstract: To evaluate the efficacy of transurethral anatomical enucleation using a 1470-nm diode laser (DiLEP) (1470 nm DiLEP) versus transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) in improving lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS) in patients with large-volume benign prostatic obstruction (BPO). In this prospective, open-label, randomized controlled trial, a total of 121 patients with large-volume BPO (> 90 ml) who visited xx Hospital between June 2022 and December 2023 were enrolled. Patients were randomly assigned using a random number table to the 1470 nm DiLEP group (n = 61) or the TURP group (n = 60). Perioperative variables, as well as functional outcomes and complications at 6 and 12 months postoperatively, were compared between the two groups. No statistically significant differences existed between groups at baseline for age, prostate volume, maximum urinary flow rate (Qmax), residual urine volume, International Prostate Symptom Score (IPSS), or quality of life (QoL) scores (P > 0.05). The 1470 nm DiLEP group demonstrated significantly shorter operative time, smaller hemoglobin decrease, lower sodium decrease, shorter catheterization duration, and shorter hospitalization compared to the TURP group (P 0.05). However, the incidence of retrograde ejaculation at 6 and 12 months postoperatively was significantly lower in the 1470 nm DiLEP group compared to the TURP group, while IIEF-5 scores were markedly improved (P < 0.05). 1470 nm DiLEP and TURP demonstrate comparable efficacy in patients with large-volume BPO. However, 1470 nm DiLEP offers superior safety, faster postoperative recovery, and minimal impact on sexual function. It is therefore more suitable for patients with large-volume BPO who maintain certain expectations for postoperative sexual function.

full_text_sections.INTRODUCTION: Introduction Benign prostatic obstruction (BPO) is a highly prevalent age-related condition among elderly men [ 1 ]. As one of the most common causes of lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS), it significantly impacts patients’ quality of life [ 2 ]. Treatment options range from medication to various surgical interventions, with selection primarily determined by prostate volume, symptom severity, and the presence of complications. Since its introduction in the 1920s, transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) has been widely recognized as the surgical gold standard for decades [ 3 ]. This procedure effectively relieves bladder outlet obstruction by removing the enlarged transitional zone tissue. Despite its proven efficacy, TURP carries significant perioperative and postoperative complications, including but not limited to: intraoperative bleeding requiring transfusion, erectile function, TUR syndrome due to fluid absorption, and postoperative urethral stricture or bladder neck contracture [ 4 , 5 ]. These inherent limitations restrict its applicability, particularly in patients with larger prostate volumes (typically defined as > 80 ml). In such cases, traditional transurethral resection often faces limitations including prolonged operative time, increased blood loss, and incomplete glandular resection leading to higher reoperation rates [ 6 ]. The pursuit of safer and more efficient surgical approaches has driven innovation in urological laser technology [ 7 ]. In recent years, high-energy medical lasers such as holmium lasers, green lasers, and semiconductor lasers have seen increasingly widespread application in BPO surgery [ 8 , 9 ]. Among these, the 1470-nm diode laser stands out due to its unique physicochemical properties [ 10 ]. This wavelength exhibits strong absorption characteristics for water molecules and hemoglobin, achieving an optimal balance between deep tissue vaporization and exceptional hemostatic capability. This dual-action mechanism efficiently ablates prostate tissue while simultaneously sealing blood vessels, thereby promising to minimize intraoperative blood loss [ 11 , 12 ]. For instance, Zhang et al. demonstrated that 1470-nm laser enucleation offered shorter catheterization time compared to plasmakinetic resection [ 9 ]. Similarly, Xiao et al. confirmed the safety profile of this laser in patients with prostates exceeding 80 ml [ 11 ]. However, despite these encouraging findings, critical knowledge gaps persist in the literature. First, most existing studies on 1470-nm laser procedures have enrolled patients with heterogeneous prostate volumes, with a mean size often below the threshold of true “large-volume” disease (typically defined as > 90–100 ml). Data regarding the specific application of 1470-nm DiLEP in a cohort with exclusively large-volume BPO (> 90 ml) remain sparse and are predominantly derived from retrospective analyses or comparisons against other laser modalities rather than the surgical gold standard, TURP. Second, while the hemostatic and fluid absorption benefits are well-documented, the precise impact of 1470-nm DiLEP on sexual function—particularly regarding erectile function scores (IIEF-5) and the incidence of retrograde ejaculation—compared to TURP in large-volume glands has not been rigorously evaluated in a prospective randomized setting. Given the elevated perioperative risks (hemorrhage, TUR syndrome, prolonged resection time) associated with TURP in prostates > 90 ml, and the growing patient expectation for preserved sexual function, high-quality evidence is urgently needed to guide surgical decision-making in this specific high-risk subgroup. Therefore, this prospective, randomized controlled trial was designed to fill this void by directly comparing transurethral 1470-nm DiLEP with conventional TURP specifically in patients with large-volume BPO (> 90 ml), with a primary focus on both perioperative safety and comprehensive postoperative functional outcomes, including sexual health.
full_text_sections.MATERIALS AND METHODS: Materials and methods Patients This study enrolled 121 patients with benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPO) from June 2022 to December 2023. Patients were randomized to either the 1470 nm DiLEP group or the TURP group. Specifically, 61 patients were randomly assigned to the 1470 nm DiLEP group and 60 patients to the TURP group. Inclusion Criteria: (1) BPO diagnosed based on typical clinical presentation, ancillary tests, and postoperative pathology; (2) Prostate volume > 90 mL; (3) Moderate-to-severe LUTS caused by BPO with a significant impact on quality of life, particularly in patients who have previously received medication but had poor response or were unable to tolerate long-term drug therapy; (4) BPO patients with the following symptoms: (i) recurrent urinary retention; (ii) recurrent hematuria; (3) Recurrent urinary tract infections; (4) Bladder stones; (5) Secondary upper urinary tract hydronephrosis (with or without renal impairment); (6) Informed consent obtained and good compliance. The exclusion criteria were as follows: Prostate volume ≤ 90 ml, history of worsening or acute exacerbation of underlying diseases in vital organs (e.g., heart, lungs, brain) within the past 3 months, Uncontrolled severe urinary tract infection or severe coagulation disorder, History of bladder cancer, prostate cancer, neurogenic bladder, urethral stricture, or prostate/urethral surgery, Use of medications to enhance sexual function, Poor compliance, inability to cooperate with treatment. This study was approved by the hospital ethics committee, and patients and their families signed informed consent forms. Study design and sample size calculation This study was designed as a prospective, single-center, open-label, randomized controlled trial. This study was approved by the hospital’s ethics committee, and the patients and their families have signed informed consent forms. Sample Size Calculation: The sample size was determined a priori based on the primary perioperative safety outcome: hemoglobin decrease (g/dL). Based on our preliminary clinical data and previously published results regarding 1470-nm laser enucleation [ 4 , 13 ], we estimated a mean difference of 0.5 g/dL in hemoglobin loss between the two groups, with an assumed standard deviation of 0.8 g/dL. To detect this difference with a two-sided significance level of 0.05 (α) and a statistical power of 80% (1-β), a minimum of 52 patients per group was required. To account for an anticipated dropout rate of approximately 15% over the 12-month follow-up period, the target enrollment was set at 60 patients per group. Randomization and Allocation Concealment: Eligible patients were randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio to either the 1470-nm DiLEP group or the TURP group. Randomization was performed using a computer-generated random number table by an independent statistician not involved in patient recruitment. Allocation concealment was ensured using sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed envelopes. The envelope was opened by a research coordinator only after the patient had completed baseline assessments and was prepared for surgery in the operating theater. Blinding: Due to the inherent differences in surgical instrumentation (laser fiber vs. resectoscope loop), blinding of the operating surgeons and patients was not possible. Therefore, this study is classified as an open-label trial. However, to minimize detection bias in the evaluation of subjective outcomes, postoperative follow-up assessments (including IPSS, QoL, and IIEF-5 questionnaires, and uroflowmetry) were conducted by dedicated research nurses who were blinded to the treatment allocation (Fig. 1 ). Fig. 1 CONSORT Flow Diagram. Flow of participants through the prospective randomized controlled trial comparing 1470-nm diode laser enucleation of the prostate (DiLEP) versus transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) for large-volume benign prostatic obstruction (> 90 ml) Treatment procedures Both groups underwent surgery performed by the same surgical team. 1470 nm DiLEP Group: This technique utilizes a 1470 nm semiconductor laser therapy system and the Great White Shark tissue shaving and pulverization system. During operation, the vaporization power was set to 150 W and the coagulation power to 50 W. The “trifoliate method” was employed for vaporization enucleation of prostate tissue. The detailed surgical techniques have been elucidated previously [ 14 ]. First, anatomical landmarks including the ureteral orifices, seminal vesicle fossa, bladder neck, and external urethral sphincter were visualized. Two marker grooves were incised bilaterally from the 5 o’clock and 7 o’clock positions of the bladder neck toward the seminal vesicle fossa, extending to the surgical capsule of the prostate. Subsequently, from the proximal seminal vesicle fossa, the glandular tissue was dissected along the surgical capsule plane while being retracted toward the bladder neck, until the entire middle lobe was pushed into the bladder. Take care to preserve the bladder neck, avoiding resection of the circular fibrous ring at its orifice, thereby maintaining the gourd-shaped structure of the prostatic apex, bladder neck, and bladder. Next, create a marked groove from the 12 o’clock position of the bladder neck toward the proximal sphincter (approximately 1.5 cm), again reaching the surgical capsule. Extend this groove bilaterally to the 11 o’clock and 1 o’clock positions. From the 5 o’clock to 1 o’clock position near the seminal tubercle, dissect along the surgical capsule plane while pushing the glandular tissue toward the bladder neck. Continue until the entire left lobe is pushed into the bladder. Repeat the same procedure for the right lobe. Meticulously control bleeding at the wound site. Insert the Great White Shark tissue shaving and pulverization system to pulverize and suction the glandular tissue pushed into the bladder. Re-examine the prostate surface and control bleeding at any active sites. During scope withdrawal, verify the integrity of the external urethral sphincter. Postoperatively, insert an F20 three-lumen Foley catheter and continue bladder irrigation with saline solution. It is important to distinguish this procedure from 1470-nm diode laser vaporization (DiVAP). The technique described herein involves blunt enucleation along the surgical capsule followed by mechanical morcellation, not pure thermal vaporization. TURP Group: This procedure followed standard TURP steps using an Olympus plasma electrosurgical resectoscope. Intraoperative settings were 280 W for resection and 120 W for coagulation. Anatomical landmarks including ureteral orifices, verumontanum, bladder neck, and external urethral sphincter were first visualized. At the 6 o’clock position, an incision groove was created in the anterior direction from the bladder neck toward the proximal verumontanum, excising the middle lobe of the prostate. Following middle lobe resection, incisions were continued along the groove margins to remove the left and right lobes sequentially from the 5 o’clock to 12 o’clock direction and from the 7 o’clock to 12 o’clock direction, respectively; Trim the prostatic apex and bladder neck to achieve complete resection of the hypertrophic tissue. After completing the resection, use an Ellick irrigator to aspirate the resected prostate specimen. After meticulous hemostasis of the prostatic wound, place an F20 three-lumen Foley catheter with appropriate traction pressure. Continue postoperative bladder irrigation with saline solution. All patients remained hospitalized with continuous bladder irrigation until hematuria resolved. The Foley catheter was removed after cessation of irrigation, and a successful trial of void (TOV) was confirmed prior to discharge. No patient was discharged on the day of surgery or with an indwelling urinary catheter. Observation of relevant indicators Record baseline data for both groups of patients prior to surgery, including age, prostate volume, maximum urinary flow rate (Qmax), residual urine volume, International Prostate Symptom Score (IPSS), and quality of life (QoL) scores. Compare intraoperative indicators between groups, including surgical duration, decrease in hemoglobin, decrease in serum sodium concentration, and number of patients requiring blood transfusion. Postoperative outcomes were compared between groups, including catheterization duration and hospital stay. Follow-up data at 6 and 12 months postoperatively were compared for Qmax, residual urine volume, IPSS, QoL, SUI, bladder neck contracture, IIEF-5 score, and incidence of retrograde ejaculation. Statistical methods SPSS 17.0 statistical software was employed. Quantitative data conforming to normal distribution were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Comparisons between groups were performed using the independent samples t-test. Qualitative data were presented as cases (%) and analyzed using the chi-square (χ²) test or Fisher’s exact test. A P value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.
full_text_sections.RESULT: Result Preoperative baseline properties of two groups As shown in Table 1 , comparison of Perioperative Data Between the Two Groups There were no statistically significant differences between the two groups in terms of age, prostate volume, Qmax, residual urine volume, IPSS, or QoL ( P > 0.05). This indicates that the clinical data from both groups are comparable, enabling effective comparison of treatment outcomes. The rate of preoperative use of BPO medical therapies was comparable between the two groups of patients. Low and balanced numbers of patients on antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy, with all drugs managed per standard perioperative bridging protocols. Similar proportion of patients presenting with an obstructive median lobe (approximately 50% in both groups). Balanced incidence of acute urinary retention requiring catheterization prior to surgery. Table 1 Baseline patient characteristics and perioperative data Clinical Features 1470 nm DiLEP ( n = 61) TURP ( n = 60) P value Age (years) 68.36 ± 4.73 67.73 ± 4.95 0.477 Prostate Volume (ml) 120.90 ± 19.16 118.90 ± 14.78 0.522 Qmax (mL/s) 5.90 ± 2.11 6.29 ± 2.36 0.339 PVR(ml) 115.10 ± 32.87 108.90 ± 32.06 0.301 IPSS 22.33 ± 4.46 21.40 ± 3.77 0.219 QoL 4.79 ± 0.90 4.55 ± 0.89 0.148 Operation time (min) 65.28 ± 9.13 76.95 ± 13.69 <0.001 Hemoglobin loss (g/dL) 1.17 ± 0.29 1.63 ± 0.38 <0.001 Sodium decrease (mmol/L) 2.88 ± 0.78 4.22 ± 0.84 <0.001 Catheter duration (hour) 48.62 ± 11.95 77.12 ± 14.82 <0.001 Hospital stay (hours) 100.10 ± 27.34 115.70 ± 28.32 0.003 Blood transfusion required 0 3 0.244 antiplatelet or anticoagulant therapy 10 12 0.652 Median lobe presence 35 31 0.540 Catheter-dependent preoperative 6 7 0.803 BPO medical therapies 50 53 0.500 Two-sample t-test for independent samples ( p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant) 1470 nm DiLEP = 1470 nm diode laser enucleation of the prostate; TURP, transurethral resection of the prostate; PVR, postvoid residual urine volume; Qmax, maximum urinary flow rate; IPSS, International Prostate Symptom Score; QoL, quality of life Comparison of perioperative variables between two groups Table 1 shows that the 1470 nm DiLEP group demonstrated significantly shorter operative times, smaller decreases in hemoglobin and serum sodium levels, shorter catheterization durations, and shorter hospital stays compared to the TURP group ( P < 0.05). Although the blood transfusion rate was higher in the TURP group than in the 1470 nm DiLEP group (3/60 vs. 0/61), no statistically significant difference existed between the two groups ( P = 0.244). Comparison of follow-up data at six and twelve months post-surgery between two patient groups As shown in Tables 2 and 3 , There were no statistically significant differences between the two groups in Qmax, residual urine volume, IPSS, or QoL ( P > 0.05). At six months postoperatively, the 1470 nm DiLEP group had 3 cases of SUI, 2 cases of bladder neck contracture, and 3 cases of urethral stricture, which were lower than the TURP group’s 7, 5, and 5 cases respectively, but none were statistically significant ( P = 0.205, 0.272). At twelve months postoperatively, re-evaluation of both groups showed 2 cases of SUI, 1 case of bladder neck contracture, and 1 case of urethral stricture in the 1470 nm DiLEP group. These figures were lower than the TURP group’s 4, 2, and 4 cases respectively, but none reached statistical significance ( P = 0.205, 0.272). Follow-up at six and twelve months postoperatively revealed significant statistical differences in IIEF-5 scores between the groups ( P = 0.046, 0.0343). The 1470 nm DiLEP group had 4 and 1 patients experiencing retrograde ejaculation at 6 and 12 months, respectively, while the TURP group had 15 and 9 patients experiencing retrograde ejaculation at the same time points. A significant statistical difference existed between the two groups ( P = 0.008, 0.02). full_text_sections.DISCUSSION: Discussion Although TURP is considered the gold standard for treating BPO, it often increases perioperative risks and complications in patients with large prostate volumes [ 15 , 16 ]. Therefore, finding a safer and more effective surgical approach for treating patients with large-volume BPO is urgently needed. This study found that 1470 nm DiLEP demonstrates excellent efficacy and safety in treating patients with large-volume BPO. Compared to TURP, 1470 nm DiLEP offers superior hemostasis and shorter operative times in large-volume BPO patients. Our findings indicate that the hemoglobin decrease in the 1470 nm DiLEP group was significantly lower than in the TURP group, with shorter procedure durations. Why does this occur? We attribute it to the following factors: 1. Differences in surgical techniques. (1) 1470 nm DiLEP locates the surgical capsule near the seminal vesicle and then excises the glandular tissue in a retrograde direction from the seminal vesicle toward the bladder neck at the capsule level, eliminating the need for repeated confirmation of the seminal vesicle location. In contrast, TURP requires more time to repeatedly confirm the seminal vesicle location during the procedure to avoid damaging the external sphincter. (2) During 1470 nm DiLEP, prostate vessels are sealed in a single step at the surgical capsule plane. In contrast, TURP involves repeated cutting through the same vessel at different levels during adenomatous tissue resection, potentially causing recurrent bleeding and clotting. Consequently, 1470 nm DiLEP results in less bleeding and more efficient hemostasis [ 17 ]. (3) The 1470 nm laser possesses excellent hemostatic and vaporization capabilities, preventing recurrent bleeding and clotting. It provides clearer intraoperative visualization, enabling more precise and rapid resection of greater volumes of hyperplastic tissue, significantly shortening procedure duration [ 18 ]. Consequently, compared to TURP, 1470 nm DiLEP results in less intraoperative bleeding and shorter surgery times when treating patients with large-volume BPO. Compared to TURP, 1470 nm DiLEP imposes a lower circulatory burden on patients with large-volume BPO. Such patients are typically older and frequently have comorbid cardiovascular diseases, making the selection of a technique with minimal circulatory impact particularly critical. Both 1470 nm DiLEP and TURP use saline as irrigation fluid, virtually eliminating the incidence of TURP syndrome (hyponatremia). However, these two procedures still differ in their impact on circulatory load. Our study found that the decrease in serum sodium levels was significantly smaller in the 1470 nm DiLEP group compared to the TURP group, indicating that more irrigation fluid entered the circulatory system during TURP. We believe this may be related to the following two factors: (1) Poor hemostasis during TURP surgery results in more open vascular stumps and longer operative times, leading to greater irrigation fluid entering the circulatory system; (2) The compromised hemostasis during TURP leads to poorer surgical visibility, increasing the likelihood of damaging the prostate’s surgical capsule and the surrounding venous sinuses. This is another significant factor contributing to greater irrigation fluid entering the circulatory system. Consequently, 1470 nm DiLEP imposes a smaller circulatory burden on patients, making it safer for those with large-volume BPO. Furthermore, follow-up results at six and twelve months post-surgery indicate that patients undergoing 1470 nm DiLEP demonstrate superior preservation of sexual function compared to those undergoing TURP, alongside a significantly reduced incidence of retrograde ejaculation. This primarily relates to damage to erection-related vessels and nerves during surgery. Due to its lower coagulation efficiency compared to 1470 nm DiLEP, TURP requires more frequent hemostasis, increasing the likelihood of damaging erection-related structures. Previous studies indicate that preserving the bladder neck effectively reduces retrograde ejaculation following prostatectomy [ 19 , 20 ]. Compared to TURP, 1470 nm DiLEP preserves the bladder neck, offering a procedural advantage. Patients in the 1470 nm DiLEP group experienced faster postoperative recovery and better short-term follow-up outcomes than those in the TURP group. This study found that patients in the 1470 nm DiLEP group had significantly shorter catheterization times and hospital stays than those in the TURP group. This is primarily attributed to reduced bleeding, shorter procedure duration, and less tissue damage during 1470 nm DiLEP surgery. Our six-month and twelve-month follow-up results showed that both groups demonstrated significant improvements in Qmax, residual urine volume, IPSS scores, and QoL compared to preoperative levels, with no significant differences between groups [ 21 ]. This indicates both techniques achieve good therapeutic outcomes for BPO. Regarding postoperative complications such as SUI and bladder neck contracture, the incidence rates were low and showed no significant differences between groups. Thus, patients in the 1470 nm DiLEP group demonstrated shorter postoperative recovery times, while achieving comparable short-term efficacy and postoperative complication rates to the TURP group. It is imperative to position the 1470-nm diode laser within the current hierarchy of established laser enucleation techniques, specifically Holmium Laser Enucleation of the Prostate (HoLEP) and Thulium Laser Enucleation of the Prostate (ThuLEP). HoLEP is widely regarded as the size-independent gold standard for endoscopic enucleation, supported by robust long-term data and guideline endorsement [ 22 ]. ThuLEP has similarly demonstrated excellent efficacy with a favorable safety profile owing to the continuous-wave nature of the thulium laser [ 23 ]. Nevertheless, the 1470-nm diode laser occupies a distinct and clinically valuable niche in the surgical armamentarium for large-volume BPO. From a tissue interaction perspective, the 1470-nm wavelength offers a unique balance. While Holmium relies on a pulsed, photothermal-mechanical effect that can occasionally obscure the capsular plane for novice surgeons, and Thulium provides a “cold-cutting” precision, the 1470-nm laser provides a pronounced simultaneous vaporization and hemostatic effect. In large-volume glands (> 90 ml), this translates to a clearer operative field and reduced necessity for frequent instrument exchanges for hemostasis compared to early experiences with HoLEP [ 24 ]. Consequently, while HoLEP and ThuLEP represent the pinnacle of enucleation efficiency, 1470-nm DiLEP serves as a pragmatic, highly effective, and safe alternative for large-volume BPO, particularly in healthcare environments prioritizing rapid patient turnover and minimal bleeding risk [ 18 ]. This study has several limitations that warrant consideration. First, this was a single-center trial with a moderate sample size, which may limit the generalizability of our findings regarding rare complications. Second, the follow-up duration was limited to 12 months; longer surveillance is required to confirm the durability of functional outcomes and the rates of late complications such as bladder neck contracture or urethral stricture. Third, and perhaps most notably, this study did not include comparison arms with other established laser enucleation techniques, namely HoLEP or ThuLEP. The choice of TURP as the comparator was intentional, as TURP remains the globally recognized gold standard and the most common comparator in randomized trials for large-volume prostates, providing a clear benchmark for safety and efficacy. Future prospective, multi-center trials comparing 1470-nm DiLEP with HoLEP and ThuLEP in a head-to-head three-arm design are eagerly awaited to fully delineate the specific advantages and indications of each laser wavelength in the management of complex large-volume BPO.
full_text_sections.CONCLUSION: Conclusion In summary, both 1470 nm DiLEP and TURP significantly improve LUTS in patients with large-volume BPO, with equivalent therapeutic efficacy. However, compared to TURP, 1470 nm DiLEP offers higher enucleation efficiency, reduced intraoperative bleeding, less impact on circulatory load, faster postoperative recovery, higher safety, and minimal effect on sexual function. Consequently, it is more suitable for patients with large-volume BPH who require preservation of sexual function.
